sources.note: Edwin Arnold died 1904 (UK life+70 expired 1974; US pre-1928). Poem is continuous; verses 2.47–2.71 are the karma-yoga teaching and description of the sthitaprajña.
Arjuna questions the stability of yoga; Krishna answers with abhyāsa (practice). Included as the mind-taming companion to the fruit-renunciation teaching of ch. 2.

Vivekananda died 1902 (US pre-1923). Sūtras on abhyāsa (practice) and vairāgya (non-attachment) as the two instruments for controlling vr̄ttis. Sūtra 1.16 defines para-vairāgya (supreme non-attachment) via knowledge of the Puruṣa.

CC0 confirmed from SuttaCentral bilara-data repo. Sutta on the five outcomes that generate nibbidā (disenchantment / vairāgya). The Buddha distinguishes the pleasure of renunciation from the filthy pleasure of popularity.

CC0 confirmed. Lute-string simile on balanced effort. The six dedications of the perfected one (renunciation, seclusion, kindness, ending of craving, ending of grasping, clarity) parallel the Gītā's framing of vairāgya. Closing rock-in-storm image parallels Arnold BG 2.70.

sources.verse_or_section: impact bias, focalism, immune neglect, durability bias
